text.title: AI IaC Red-Flags Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules every red-flag review uses. Hard rules; reviewer MUST scan each flag class on every AI-IaC PR.

rule.statement: The 8-pattern catalogue (`templates/redflag-catalogue.md`) is the authoritative list. The reviewer MUST scan every flag class on every AI-IaC PR. AI assistants are FORBIDDEN from editing the catalogue; CODEOWNERS protection enforces this.
rule.rationale: The catalogue's value depends on its stability. If the AI can edit the rules that catch it, the catalogue is theatre. CODEOWNERS protection is the structural defence.

rule.statement: Provider schema (Terraform Registry / Pulumi schema / Helm values JSON-schema) is the source of truth for argument names, types, and deprecation status. The reviewer / scanner MUST consult schema on every flagged argument. Decisions based on "the AI said" or memory are REJECTED.
rule.rationale: AI assistants emit deprecated arguments at high rate (`aws_s3_bucket.acl` post v4.0, `kubernetes_pod_v1.spec.dns_policy` legacy values). Schema is the only mechanism that catches deprecation without manual research.

rule.statement: The scan MUST run regex pre-pass for fast pattern detection (secret-looking strings, "TODO" placeholders, "REPLACE-ME"), then AST-level validation for argument-level hallucination. Regex-only is insufficient; AST-only is too slow. The two-stage pass is non-negotiable.
rule.rationale: Regex catches secret-shaped strings and obvious placeholders in < 1s. AST validation catches structural errors but takes 5-30s. Run regex first, gate AST on regex pass, and the reviewer waits seconds, not minutes.

rule.statement: Any string that matches a secret-detection regex (high-entropy, key-shaped prefixes like `AKIA`, `sk-`, `ghp_`, `xoxb-`) MUST flag `block` regardless of whether the value is real. The reviewer MUST verify the value is not a real secret AND scrub it from git history if it is.

rationale.source: Faion sibling rule `sec-secrets-defense-in-depth`; real-world AI-chat secret leaks

rule.rationale: AI assistants paste real credentials from chat context into IaC files when the operator includes secrets in the prompt. The block-on-shape rule prevents the most common path from chat to committed secret.

rule.statement: Resources MUST carry tags as defined in `tag-policy.yaml` (typically: owner, environment, cost-center, data-classification). AI-generated resources frequently omit tags or use generic placeholders ("ai-generated", "default"). The scanner MUST flag every untagged or placeholder-tagged resource.
rule.rationale: Untagged resources are uncountable. Cost attribution, security blast-radius, lifecycle management all depend on tag presence. AI assistants do not invent organisation-specific tags; the policy file is the source of truth.

section.title: The eight red flags (catalogue summary)

list.item: RF-01 hallucinated provider argument (schema says no such field)
RF-02 invented resource name (`aws_route53_zone_record` instead of `aws_route53_record`)
RF-03 drifted version constraint (required_providers unpinned, helm chart version range too wide)
RF-04 copied secret verbatim (high-entropy string, key-shaped prefix)
RF-05 for_each / count misuse (for_each over a list of unstable keys, count where for_each is correct)
RF-06 dangling output (output references a resource attribute that no longer exists)
RF-07 count-of-list shape mismatch (helm values list vs object, terraform list(object) vs object)

section.reference: The 8-pattern catalogue with concrete examples per flag.

example.title: RF-01 + RF-03 in one diff
example.note: RF-03 detected by regex (no version field). RF-01 detected by AST (schema lookup: `acl` removed; replaced by `aws_s3_bucket_acl` resource).

example.title: Diff after red-flag repair
example.note: RF-01, RF-03, RF-08 all satisfied. Schema-correct resource decomposition, version pinned, tags present.
